original_hypothesis.title: Excess ETH Permanently Locked in EntityForging Contract
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: forgeWithListed checks `msg.value >= forgingFee` but only distributes exactly forgingFee (split between nukeFund and forger owner). Any excess ETH (msg.value - forgingFee) remains permanently locked in the EntityForging contract with no withdrawal mechanism, receive function, or sweep function.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Unlike mintToken which refunds excess, forgeWithListed keeps surplus. EntityForging has no fallback for trapped ETH recovery. Over time, accumulated excess ETH becomes permanently irrecoverable.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(msg.value >= forgingFee) with no refund of (msg.value - forgingFee); devFee and forgerShare sum to forgingFee, not msg.value

preconditions: User sends msg.value > forgingFee to forgeWithListed

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Overpayment accepted but not refunded; no withdrawal function
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: devFee = forgingFee/taxCut, forgerShare = forgingFee-devFee; excess = msg.value-forgingFee is trapped
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No refund logic, no sweep function, no require(msg.value == forgingFee)

analysis_questions: Is there any receive() or fallback() that could extract trapped ETH?
Can owner withdraw trapped ETH through any mechanism?
How much ETH could accumulate over time?
validation_requirements: Verify contract balance increases by excess amount

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Users accidentally overpaying forging fees lose the excess permanently. Over time, ETH accumulates in the EntityForging contract with no recovery mechanism.
prerequisites: User sends more ETH than the forging fee

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Direct loss of user ETH on every overpayment; cumulative loss over protocol lifetime
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any user who overpays forging fees

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test for overpayment scenario in forgeWithListed

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable via contract balance vs expected balance

validation_result.impact_summary: Any ETH sent above the forging fee in forgeWithListed is permanently locked in the EntityForging contract with no recovery mechanism.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The forgeWithListed function in EntityForging.sol accepts msg.value >= forgingFee but only distributes exactly forgingFee (split into devFee sent to nukeFundAddress and forgerShare sent to forger owner). The excess amount (msg.value - forgingFee) remains in the contract. There is no receive(), fallback(), withdraw(), or sweep function in EntityForging.sol that could recover this trapped ETH. This is a clear code defect compared to TraitForgeNft.mintToken which explicitly refunds excess payment.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Lines 107-109 of EntityForging.sol show `require(msg.value >= forgingFee, 'Insufficient fee for forging')` which accepts overpayment. Lines 120-126 show that only `devFee` (forgingFee/taxCut) and `forgerShare` (forgingFee - devFee) are distributed, summing to exactly forgingFee. No refund logic exists anywhere in the function. The entire contract has no ETH extraction mechanism.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: devFee + forgerShare = forgingFee. If msg.value > forgingFee, the difference (msg.value - forgingFee) remains in the contract with no way to extract it. Compare to mintToken in TraitForgeNft.sol which refunds excess: `uint256 excessPayment = msg.value - mintPrice; if (excessPayment > 0) { (bool refundSuccess, ) = msg.sender.call{ value: excessPayment }(''); }`

calling_sequence: 1. User calls forgeWithListed with msg.value > forgingFee
3. Line 108: require(msg.value >= forgingFee) passes (e.g., msg.value = 1 ETH)
8. Total distributed: 0.1 ETH. Excess locked: 0.9 ETH
9. Function ends with no refund logic
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The complete function distributes exactly forgingFee via two .call{value}() transfers (devFee to nukeFundAddress at line 126, forgerShare to forgerOwner at line 128). The excess (msg.value - forgingFee) is never touched. EntityForging has no receive/fallback function and no withdrawal mechanism anywhere in the contract.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deployment scripts show EntityForging is deployed with only _traitForgeNft address parameter. No withdrawal or sweep function is configured post-deployment. The deploy-all task sets nukeFundAddress but adds no ETH recovery mechanism.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: A user calls forgeWithListed with msg.value = 1 ETH when the listing fee is 0.1 ETH. The require(msg.value >= forgingFee) passes. 0.01 ETH goes to nukeFund, 0.09 ETH goes to forger owner. The remaining 0.9 ETH is trapped in EntityForging forever. This can happen accidentally (user miscalculates), or through UI bugs. Over time, accumulated excess ETH is permanently irrecoverable since the contract has no withdrawal function, no receive() function, and no administrative sweep function.
validation_result.impact_assessment: Direct ETH loss for any user who overpays forging fees. The severity is MEDIUM because: (1) the loss requires user error (sending more than needed), but (2) there is zero recovery possibility, and (3) the fix is trivial (either require exact payment or refund excess). This is a genuine code defect regardless of whether exploitation is intentional.
recommended_mitigations: Option A (strict): Change require(msg.value >= forgingFee) to require(msg.value == forgingFee) to reject overpayment

Option B (refund): After distributing devFee and forgerShare, refund excess: uint256 excess = msg.value - forgingFee; if (excess > 0) { (bool refundSuccess,) = msg.sender.call{value: excess}(''); require(refundSuccess, 'Refund failed'); }
Option C (additional safety): Add an owner-only sweep function to recover any accidentally trapped ETH
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy EntityForging with a mock TraitForgeNft. 2. List a forger token with fee = 0.1 ETH. 3. Call forgeWithListed with msg.value = 1 ETH. 4. Assert that EntityForging.balance == 0.9 ETH after the transaction. 5. Confirm no function exists to withdraw the 0.9 ETH.
uncertainties: The exact frequency of user overpayment is unknown, but the code path is reachable and the defect is real regardless of frequency
additional_testing_needed: Integration test confirming excess ETH accumulates in EntityForging contract
Verify no inherited function from OpenZeppelin contracts provides an ETH withdrawal path
Test with various overpayment amounts to confirm all excess is trapped
